# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. A Fuck You to Corporate America

**Score:** 71
**Body:** My company just announced its twelvetieth round of redundancies and I am feeling more than a little hostile to big corporations. Any suggestions for movies that show the little guy winning out against a big company?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They Live
2. Office Space
3. Sorry To Bother You, I think?
4. Wanted


Office Space


Falling Down
  - Time has shown that Milton was the only one with the right idea.
5. American Psycho (an allegory for corporate greed)
6. Nine to Five
7. Erin Brockovitch
8. Michael Clayton. “Do I look like I’m negotiating?”
9. I saw this morning in the news: ***Amazon to lay off as many as 30,000 corporate jobs***

I think starting tomorrow, That's a buttload

## 2. Movies where we have to use our brain

**Score:** 60
**Body:** Can you tell me some movie or something which actually requires brain to watch, because most shows are pretty dumb, like they try to over-explain everything. In case the fucking stupid audience is sitting there watching, especially the Indian television, they over-exaggerate the detail. But can you tell me where a movie or something where we have to use our brain, and it's really for smart people or like that basically.



### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ~~Otnemem~~ Memento
2. Coherence

Primer

Perfect Blue
  - I've watched Primer multiple times, and still have to refer to the wiki diagram
  - God damn was Coherence good. My only regret is that I can’t watch it again for the first time with virgin eyes.
3. Anything by David Lynch. 2046. Everything Everywhere All At Once. Parasite. Sunshine. Annihilation. Portrait of a Lady on Fire. Power of the Dog. I’m Thinking of Ending Things. Double. Swiss Army Man. Sinners. Black Swan. Being John Malkovich. Synecdoche New York.
4. “12 Monkeys” is very demanding.
5. Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy
6. I was just having this conversation with friends! I highly recommend Dark City.
7. Everything written by Charlie Kaufman
8. All the recent (ish) John le Carré adaptations:

The Constant Gardener (2005, Oscar for Rachel Weisz)

Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy (2011, stacked cast, impossibly complicated and utterly brilliant)

A Most Wanted Man (2014, Philip Seymour Hoffman's final lead role)

## 3. a comfort movie

**Score:** 20
**Body:** one that drowns at your surroundings and is wholesome and beautiful 

i would like one that is light hearted and as a good soundtrack and makes you feel unreal 

cartoon or live action idm 

:):) 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I kinda love the feeling of Ratatouille on a rainy Sunday in the fall…
2. * King Fu Panda - the first one (Jack Black is a national treasure)
* Spirited Away
* Perfect Days - (kind-hearted)
* School of Rock (repeating it here because it's so good)
* The Incredibles
* Inside Out
3. Spirited away
4. * Nacho Libre (2006)

* A Good Year (2006)

* School of Rock (2003)

* About Time (2013)

* The Legend of Bagger Vance (2000)
5. The Holdovers
6. Babe (1995) and any of the Muppet movies!
7. My comfort movies are Empire Records, Drop Dead Gorgeous, Josie & The Pussycats, 100 Cigarettes, Spice World and when I’m sick, Pacific Rim.
8. Ive always liked Crazy Stupid Love
9. You are looking for Momma Mia



Other great ones are
Clueless.

Legally Blonde.

And now the Barbie Movie

Ps: also Lego Batman.
10. Rocky Horror Picture Show

Almost Famous

## 4. Which film most realistically represents prison life?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** 
I have a special fondness for movies about prison life. I’ve realized I haven’t seen a good one in a long time. So, I’m looking for movie suggestions that accurately depict the true reality of prison life.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sing Sing - many of the actors are ex-prisoners.
2. Un prophète 2009
  - Yes this plus The Night of
3. Starred Up

"Proud to be your dad."
4. Sing Sing 
5. Face/Off, easy.
  - With the magnetic boots yessssss
6. Starred Up


A Prayer Before Dawn 
7. Not a movie but BBC series “Time” with Sean Bean is excellent, and available on the HBO app
8. Midnight Express

Escape from Dannemora

## 5. Movies like summer of 84 giving you that 80s feel

**Score:** 17
**Body:** 
Movies like title or that give the feeling like stranger things. 

No cell phones, just kids/people living life. 

80s films are some of my favourite. They don’t have to actually be from that era just set in that time. It just gives me such a comfy cosy feeling you know? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fast Times At Ridgemont High
2. However the best 80's movie that fits this in my opinion is Better Off Dead.
3. The Wedding Singer! It's so 80s I forget it wasn't made in the 80s!
4. The Breakfast Club

16 Candles

Say Anything

Some Kind of Wonderful

The Lost Boys
5. Grease     

Cocktail      

Pretty in pink      

Ferris Beuller’s day off     

Bright lights, big city      

Big      

Adventures in babysitting     

Flashdance       

TopGun
6. Super 8 - ('79 but close enough)

The Monster squad

It

The Black phone

Stand by me

The Goonies

The 'Burbs

The lost boys
7. Summer School
  - Very underrated movie. One of my favorites.
8. Desperately Seeking Susan 

Valley Girl
9. Night of The Comet.(85)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. How come Gus Van Sant’s Psycho isn’t remembered as the giant middle finger to Hollywood that it was?

**Score:** 1754
**Body:** So the story goes that GVS was fed up with studio interference and released a sarcastically scene for scene, shot for shot, line for line remake of psycho.

It got a lot of criticism at the time for not doing anything original, but it was a deliberate choice by him from getting tired of being told no at every step.

And absolutely no one remembers it for that, or at all for that matter.

I mean it’s pretty fucking punk rock if you asked me.


### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This reminds me of when Werner Herzog made a sequel to a movie he never saw. 
  - Additionally, there’s this wonderful little tidbit from the [Wikipedia article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bad_Lieutenant:_Port_of_Call_New_Orleans).

>Abel Ferrara, director of the 1992 film, has been quoted by various media outlets as being very angry about the film. After it was first announced, Ferrara was quoted as saying "As far as remakes go, ... I wish these people die in Hell. I hope they're all in the same streetcar, and it blows up." When asked later for his response to Ferrara's statements, Herzog stated that he does not know who Ferrara is, saying "I've never seen a film by him. I have no idea who he is."
    - "I don't know her."
    - I don't think Herzog has ever said something I don't find inherently hilarious.
      - “The rules for the humans are do not disturb or hold up the penguin. Stand still and let him go on his way. And here, he's heading off into the interior of the vast continent. With 5,000 kilometers ahead of him, he's heading towards certain death.”

LOL 🤣
2. Ok. Hear me out here, while I do believe Vince Vaughn was miscast as Norman due to his physicality. After-all, a large imposing Norman Bates hits different than a meek, slender iteration. 

That said, I also think he was genuinely creepy and did well with his performance. You almost forget how huge the guy is, because his demeanor was so genuinely timid.
  - Seeing a 6'5" tall Mrs Bates leap across the root cellar at the end of the film was properly funny.
3. The explicit masturbation scene in the remake would beg to differ (as far as it being a shot for shot remake is concerned).
  - He was shooting his shot.
4. The masterbation scene for sure wasn’t in the original. Lol. (That’s all I remember from this movie)

## 2. I might be late to the party, but hot damn this movie is incredible.

**Score:** 1062
**Body:** Knowing a little bit about the characters background makes this movie soo much more enjoyable!

Watched it for the first tine today and I am honestly blown away.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m your Huckleberry
  - *YOU TELL EM' IM COMIN', AND HELL'S COMIN' WITH ME!!!"*
    - ***Go ahead, skin it.  Skin that smokewagon and see what happens.***
      - *ROADHOUSE*

wait-
  - Why Johnny Ringo you look like someone just walked over your grave
2. Never too late and yes it’s damn near perfect.
3. "you're a daisy if you do"

Yeah it's a great movie easily top 5 westerns.
4. "I've got 2 guns. One for the each of ya."
5. Are you going to do something or just stand there and bleed?
6. Val Kilmer as Doc should’ve won him an Oscar. The movie is simply only good without him but WITH him it makes it fantastic

## 3. Favorite Broken Lizard movie that’s not Super Troopers?

**Score:** 325

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Beerfest!
  - DAS BOOT!
    - Becks!
  - God damn brewery! You know that brewery makes 10,000 bottles of beer a day. I drink 45 of them, and I'm the asshole!
2. I think this is just my favorite BL film
  - Me too. Pina colada-burg is way better than fuckin margaritaville
3. Peen-a-lope.
4. Slammin’ Salmon

The scene where one of them quits to work in Hollywood was pretty amazing.
  - Which one of you is Guy…Meat-drapes?
5. Slammin salmon

## 4. its aight

**Score:** 292
**Body:** I think this just hasn‘t aged very well. All the scenes with the son are terrible his acting is horrible. The opening is the best part of the movie. Viggos acting is very good. Ed Harris plays a very one dimensional character which is quite funny considering his famous outrage during that interview. And also I mean the writing could have been better "Any last words?" "..I should have killed you back in Philly" Its very short so there just isn‘t much to it. Its not bad. Its good. 3.5 Stars on Letterboxd. I mean I have just heard the title thrown around a lot. I liked William Hurt at the end but jesus christ an Oscar nom? Quiet year? I mean there just isn‘t much to say about it its aight.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This movie had some gritty fucking scenes
2. The ultra rare 69 scene
  - Cronenberg proudly states in the director's commentary that it's the first ever depicted in a Hollywood film.
    - Haha. It’s almost like he was dared to do it or something. But honestly it’s kind of a sweet husband wife sex scene, much less gratuitous than it sounds on paper
3. Apparently this was the last movie to get a vhs release so that’s kinda interesting.
  - And apparently from a comment on here the first 69. Lol. Great movie….Joey…
    - You must be mistaken, it's Tom.
4. Ed Harris was so good as an asshole
5. This is a great movie
6. “How do you fuck that up?”
Still a line I use every day.

## 5. Is range that important

**Score:** 200
**Body:** This guy is obsessed with range as if you can't be a good or a great actor without it. But in reality, as long as an actor does a great job playing a certain type of roles, it shouldn't matter if they have a huge range or not.

https://youtu.be/_uj9ftbpZ9I

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is range bait.
  -  I see what you did there
2. Being typecast can be a very lucrative career path.
  - It literally saved Giancarlo Esposito's life
3. Range, Range against the dying of the light.
4. Eh. If they’re trying to stretch their range and simp cannot, yeah. Looks and feels weird seeing someone flail. 

 But just not having it being bad? No I don’t think so. Some people are good at one thing.  Let them have that thing
5. Viggo Mortensen slays them all. After all, he's been a *ranger*...
6.  I actually strongly believe that range isn’t the most important thing. Obviously it’s incredibly impressive when an actor has great range. 

But some people have a charisma or do one thing really well and that’s just as important to me.
7. Naw.  Ben Affleck is mostly Ben Affleck in every role he plays, but I still love to watch him.
8. Not that important. Keanu Reeves is great and I love watching his movies, but nobody is accusing him of having any range.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Patlabor 2 and the illusion of peace

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Patlabor 2: The Movie is a 1993 animé film directed by Mamoru Oshii, most famous for his next film, Ghost in the Shell. It's based on the series Patlabor, about a police unit specialising in crimes involving "labors": giant humanoid robots mostly used in construction. I've been thinking about the film lately because of how its themes relate to the present situation with Ukraine.

Patlabor 2 feels to me like a film that is straining somewhat against its source. The sophisticated story and themes sit a little awkwardly against a fairly light-hearted animé about police using giant robots, and the labor unit being so central to the plot. The robots are mostly in the background though, aside from the finale.

Those issues aside, the film shows off Oshii's skills as a director (and the skills of the artists, animators and others involved). Much like Ghost in the Shell, the film takes time to look at the city it's set in, from the perspective of the people who live in it. A stand-out scene shows JSDF troops and tanks on the streets of Tokyo, juxtaposing the military and its hardware against waving schoolchildren, brightly lit shop windows and gently falling snow. Another stand-out scene is an exceptionally tense air-to-air interception.

The opening scene of the film sets up the critique of Japanese society and politics made by the film's antagonist.

A Japanese-crewed multi-legged tank is operating somewhere in South-East Asia as part of a UN mission. As potentially hostile forces approach, the commander asks repeatedly for permission to engage, only to be denied, a delay which leads to the destruction of the unit. The Japanese forces are ill-prepared; they've failed to consider the situation realistically and make hard decisions (many stories would lean into the view that the commanders didn't value the lives of their troops, but I don't think Patlabor 2 does this, or at least, not in the usual way).

Now I'm not Japanese and I'm sure I'm missing a lot here, but I know enough about Japanese history and culture to make some sense of what the film is talking about.

After WW2 pacifism became deeply embedded in Japan. The country's constitution set tight limits on the use of military force, and this was widely supported. I don't think it was until the 2000s that Japanese forces were deployed in a military operation overseas (logistical support for ISAF, iirc).

However right from the early days of the Cold War Japan was drawn into global conflicts and alliances. It was an important staging post in the Korean War, and Japan became a close ally of the United States. Japan's role influenced the country's economy, from the conglomerates manufacturing military vehicles to the small businesses serving US service personnel stationed in or on R&R in Japan. The Japanese politician who identifies (whether ideologically or corruptly) completely with US military & industrial interests is a familiar figure in animé.

In the critique Patlabor 2 presents, Japanese people live under the illusion of peace. They are able to live comfortable lives, feeling good about their country's disavowal of violence, while ignoring its true role in and connections to actual wars, or the potential to be pulled into future wars. They don't share in the uncertainty and the suffering. They don't treat the real war and violence around the world, and the potential of war to affect Japan, with the seriousness they merit, and Japanese society and politics are fundamentally unprepared to deal with the effects of war.

While the film doesn't endorse the antagonist's views, it does show a Japan with a dysfunctional political system ill-prepared to deal with a crisis. The rebuttal of the antagonist's philosophy is probably more subtle and soft than their (very explicit) articulation.

Today, the film makes a striking comparison when looking at many European countries with regards to Ukraine. We're active participants in this war, providing materiel, training and intelligence to Ukraine. In terms of strict legality we are belligerents and Russia would be within its rights to attack us, but it chooses not to, aside for some modest sabotage and some rather more serious cyber attacks. (Well, "within its rights" if we ignore the illegality of Russia starting the war in the first place, but let's not get sidetracked.) 

We participate in this war - and defence spending does get raised - but we don't really consider ourselves to be involved in it. Mostly our politics, societies and economies run as normal. Are they as fragile (and complacent?) as Japan in Patlabor 2? Do we view our countries to be at peace? (Or post war-on-terror have we all got used to a neverending state that's neither really war nor peace?)

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. All three of them are masterpieces. Nobody does a quick city montage better than him and the way he uses light and music during said sequences is the best. He gets the spotlight for GITS but should get the praise for the former movies. They’re some of the best procedurals ever. Cannot praise enough.

## 3. Casual Discussion Thread (October 29, 2025)

**Score:** 3
**Body:** General Discussion threads threads are meant for more casual chat; a  place to break most of the frontpage rules. Feel free to ask for  recommendations, lists, homework help; plug your site or video essay;  discuss tv here, or any such thing.

**There is no 180-character minimum for top-level comments in this thread.**

**Follow us on:**

* [Letterboxd](http://letterboxd.com/truefilmreddit/)
* [Twitter](https://twitter.com/truefilmreddit)

The sidebar has a wealth of information, including the subreddit  rules, our killer wiki, all of our projects... If you're on a mobile  app, click the "(i)" button on our frontpage.

Sincerely,

David

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hi all!

My buddy and I started a film podcast back in July called Hard Ticket, a premise where we pick a double feature and pit the two films “against each other” in a way, discussing surprising similarities and meaningful differences. The podcast stresses thematic analysis, as well as context; my co-host and I joke that it’s firmly middle-brow. You’re getting personality and jokes from us, but we also hope our analysis of the similarities between what may initially seem like disparate features in a matchup yield an interesting discussion and a unique way of looking at the subject matter.

If you’re interested, I’d love to hear some feedback from film peeps. We’re also always interested in featuring guests; veteran cinephiles would be awesome to have on, especially if you have podcast or broadcasting experience. We’ve got some good feedback from friends and family, as well as some randoms who’ve picked it up through Spotify, Reddit, and Letterboxd. This month, we’ve been doing horror movie matchups, with the latest being *Hausu* vs. *Evil Dead II*: something for the arthouse crowd and cult aficionados to sink their teeth into.

You can listen on Spotify [here](https://spotify.link/wbcZCCAyQXb). Thanks for your time!

## 4. When One Face Ruins the Whole Story, Which Film Did You Walk Away From?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Have you ever felt pulled toward a film, its story, its themes, its emotional promise, only to hesitate because one actor shattered the illusion? Not because they lacked talent, but because their presence broke something personal: a memory, a bias, a credibility thread you couldn’t ignore. Sometimes it’s not the story that fails us, it’s the face telling it. And that’s okay. Emotional investment is fragile, and cinema is personal. What’s the film you couldn’t bring yourself to watch, because one performance disrupted the connection?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not saying it was a bad movie at all, but that scene in Ray when Jamie Foxx is singing Hit the Road Jack, in just a white undershirt, I just can’t help but wonder how much Ray Charles can bench press.
2. Supersize Me after we figured out Spurlock was an alcoholic. And that most of his symptoms in his films and shows were actually from being an alcoholic.


Turns out it wasn't the Big Macs doing all the heavy lifting. Although, short term, probably didn't help.
3. The big sick . I could never buy the main character pulling that girl to the point that I got upset. Like he directed the movie and his vanity didn’t let him cast a more believable lead
  - Being at least a certain type of stand-up comedian, and I think we can say that Kumail Nanjiani is this type, the act has a lot to do with projecting an outsized confidence / charisma. I’d say rather that they played this down in the movie, and Nanjiani’s exploits rizzing up lots of ‘on a pure looks basis’ out-of-his-league women.
  - What are you talking about? Google Emily V. Gordon. Zoe Kazan is certainly more classically attractive than her, but they are in the same league!

## 5. A Girl Walks Home Alone At Night is not a great movie

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I finished watching Let The Right One In, and thought it was one of the best movies I've seen in years. So I searched the internet for movies recommended similar to it. The near unanimous opinion was for A Girl Walks Home Alone At Night.

I bought it, watched it, and having just finished have to ask; why is this movie so loved?

I can understand the appreciation for the monotone colors, black and white, good and evil. The soundtrack was hyped too but didn't do much besides add noise to the nothing this movie was.

I can also understand the girl boss undertones. When the girl confronts the father and literally jumps into scene straight out of SNL Spanish inquisition style, I laughed out loud.

Maybe I'm too dense to understand "cinema" but this movie sucked. Can someone explain why this is supposed to be a near masterpiece? Thought Let The Right One In was leagues ahead in every aspect, but maybe I'm comparing apples to oranges despite the recommendations. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I can see why A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night might be recommended if you like Let the Right One In even though they're very different films. 

The big similarity is that both films the vampire is someone who would normally be perceived as vulnerable. They use this to approach and present the vampire mythos in unconventional ways. Both vampires are sympathetic - up to a point.

However I'd say that Let the Right One In is more of a conventional story while A Girl Walks Home is more of a mood piece. Let the Right One In starts with the question: "what if an old vampire had the body of a child?" and then tries to fit that into an otherwise realistic story.

The story of A Girl Walks Home is more dreamlike. It's not one that has a clear logic to it or that's based on strong character development. It's set in a kind of nowhere place detached from any wider world. Scenes are sometimes set up more for style than logic (why a date at the power station? For the lighting!). Things happen; trying to understand them all clearly or literally is a fool's errand (who can really understand a vampire, or a world that contains vampires?).

For me, this works. The film is strange and a bit mysterious, it's cool, a little sexy, it looks great and sounds good. It keeps you on your toes not knowing quite what to expect from it. I've not seen anyone suggest it's a near masterpiece, but I do agree with the praise it received.

I doubt it's your ability to understand cinema that meant it didn't work for you. A dreamlike, mood piece like A Girl Walks Home tends to either connect with you and pull you in or it doesn't. For example, if you didn't like the soundtrack, then it's going to be difficult to enjoy the film. Or, to pick another example, I've seen it compared to David Lynch, a director whose films have never clicked for me.
  - Great response, I appreciate it.

I thought Let The Right One In was more about a lonely young boy with no friends that happens to find one through violence. How a man will give his life to serve a woman he loves, that shows love back.

It was interesting wondering if the girl was legitimately falling for the boy or if she was intent on finding another familiar through manipulation. Hard to tell, that's what makes it so good.

I would disagree that A Girl Walks Home keeps you on your toes, I was more wondering what is happening and why there's a weird dude dancing with a balloon out of nowhere. I think you're right about it being a mood film, guess I wasnt expecting it coming from LTROI.

Think maybe mood films aren't my thing, but Enter the Void is sometimes considered one and I love that movie.
2. 1) Historically *Hollywood* movies tell stories about male vampires who prey on women. These movies belie or mask the predator/prey element by presenting the vampire's predation as love - which is deeply gross. Let the right one in reengages this dynamic by making the vampire a woman trapped in a child's body - which is still pretty gross.

A girl walks home alone at night truly reverses the dynamic by having a female vampire fall in love with a man who falls in love with her despite understanding her monstrousness. This is all really exciting for some parts of the movie's potential audience because it's taking on the tired conventions of the vampire genre and the romance genre.

2) The movie is set in Iran. If you don't know, Iran's government has implemented a wide range of laws restricting women's ability to be present in public with any kind of equality because member of its dominant religion are terrified of women. This movie seizes that fear and embodies it in a way that plays with the notion of women's power, men's arrogance, and the interplay between an intrusion of western culture in a country that's terrified of western culture.

3) The movie is aurally and visually gorgeous.

4) It doesn't look or sound like anything else that's being made.

---
